title: Inclusive = In fact (clarify)
definition: Use 'in fact' when you want to emphasize that something is true, especially if the listener might doubt it.
opt3: "Inclusive, fui eu que fiz isso." → "In fact, I was the one who did that."
opt4: "Inclusive, ele mora aqui há 10 anos." → "In fact, he has lived here for 10 years."
example3: 'In fact' is stronger than 'actually' – it insists on truth.

title: Inclusive = Actually (soft correction)
definition: Use 'actually' to correct a misunderstanding or introduce new, slightly surprising information.
opt3: "Inclusive, ela não gosta de café." → "Actually, she doesn't like coffee."
opt4: "Inclusive, eu estava lá também." → "Actually, I was there too."
example3: 'Actually' is very common in spoken English; it softens the contradiction.

title: Inclusive = What's more (add stronger point)
definition: Use 'what's more' to add a second point that is even stronger or more important than the first.
opt3: "O preço é alto. Inclusive, nem tem garantia." → "The price is high. What's more, it doesn't even come with a warranty."
opt4: "Ela é inteligente. Inclusive, fala cinco línguas." → "She's smart. What's more, she speaks five languages."

title: Inclusive = Even (surprising example)
definition: Use 'even' before a noun or verb to show that something is surprising or extreme.
opt3: "Todo mundo riu, inclusive o professor." → "Everyone laughed, even the teacher."
opt4: "Ele acorda cedo até nos fins de semana, inclusive no domingo." → "He wakes up early even on weekends, even on Sunday."
example3: Place 'even' directly before the surprising element.

title: Inclusive = Including (neutral list)
definition: Use 'including' when you simply list items without any surprise or emphasis.
opt3: "Comprei frutas, inclusive maçãs e bananas." → "I bought fruit, including apples and bananas."
opt4: "Convidamos todos, inclusive as crianças." → "We invited everyone, including the children."
example3: Never use 'even' for neutral lists – it would sound odd.

title: Inclusive = Besides / On top of that (additional reason)
definition: Use 'besides' or 'on top of that' to add another reason, often making a previous argument stronger.
opt3: "Não quero sair. Inclusive, está chovendo." → "I don't want to go out. Besides, it's raining."
opt4: "Já estou atrasado. Inclusive, perdi o ônibus." → "I'm already late. On top of that, I missed the bus."
example3: 'Besides' is often used to add a final, convincing reason.

title: Inclusive = As a matter of fact (formal)
definition: A more formal version of 'in fact', used in writing or formal speech.
opt3: "Inclusive, fui promovido ontem." → "As a matter of fact, I was promoted yesterday."
opt4: "Inclusive, ela é a fundadora da empresa." → "As a matter of fact, she is the founder of the company."
example3: Use this in business emails or presentations.

title: Inclusive = Not to mention (obvious point)
definition: Use 'not to mention' to add something that should already be obvious, reinforcing the argument.
opt3: "Ele é um péssimo motorista. Inclusive, já bateu o carro três vezes." → "He's a terrible driver, not to mention he's crashed the car three times."
opt4: "O hotel era sujo. Inclusive, tinha baratas." → "The hotel was dirty, not to mention there were cockroaches."
example3: 'Not to mention' is often used for negative examples.

title: Even – position rules
definition: 'Even' comes directly before the word/phrase you want to emphasize as surprising.
opt3: "Even John came to the party." (surprising that John came)
opt4: "John even cleaned the house." (surprising action)
correct_answer: "John came even though he was sick." (different structure – 'even though' is a conjunction)
example3: Do not say 'Even John came to the party' if you mean 'John came to the party, even.' – position changes meaning.

title: In fact vs Actually – subtle difference
definition: 'In fact' emphasizes that something is true (often counter to belief). 'Actually' corrects a misconception gently.
opt3: "People think he's rich. In fact, he's broke." (strong contrast)
opt4: "Are you tired? "Actually
example4: Use 'in fact' to double down; use 'actually' to pivot.

title: Avoiding false friends with 'including'
definition: Portuguese 'inclusive' often means 'even' or 'in fact' – do not default to 'including'.
opt3: ❌ 'Including, I was there.' ✅ 'In fact, I was there.'
opt4: ❌ 'He's smart, including funny.' ✅ 'He's smart – even funny.'
example3: Ask yourself: 'Am I listing items?' If yes → 'including'. If emphasizing → 'in fact' or 'even'.

title: What's more vs Moreover vs Furthermore
opt3: "It's cheap. What's more, it's durable." (casual)
opt4: "The proposal is innovative. Moreover, it is feasible." (business)
example3: Match the register to your audience.

title: Inclusive substitute flowchart
definition: Use this table to choose the right word.
opt2: Are you clarifying/correcting? → In fact / Actually
opt3: Adding a stronger point? → What's more / Moreover
opt4: Surprising example? → Even
example4: When in doubt, 'actually' works in most spoken situations.

title: Top 3 mistakes with inclusive
opt3: ❌ 'Inclusive, I like pizza.' ✅ 'Actually, I like pizza.'
opt4: ❌ 'She speaks three languages, inclusive French.' ✅ 'She speaks three languages, including French.' (or 'even French')
example4: Never start an English sentence with 'Inclusive' as a discourse marker.

title: Identify the function of 'inclusive'
question_text: 'Inclusive, fui eu que fiz isso.' – What is the speaker doing?
opt3: Adding a stronger point
opt4: Giving a neutral example
explanation: The speaker emphasizes it was ME who did it – likely correcting someone.

title: Choose best substitute (stronger point)
question_text: 'O carro é velho. Inclusive, nem liga mais.' – Best replacement for 'Inclusive'?
opt3: What's more
opt4: Actually
explanation: The second sentence is a stronger argument (car doesn't even start).

title: Neutral list or surprise?
question_text: 'Comprei legumes, inclusive cenouras.' – Should you use 'even' or 'including'?
opt3: In fact
opt4: What's more
explanation: This is a plain list – no surprise, so 'including' is correct.

title: Formal writing choice
question_text: In a business report, which is most appropriate for 'inclusive' when adding a point?
opt3: Even
opt4: Actually

title: Spot the error
opt3: Even the teacher laughed.
opt4: What's more, it's free.
explanation: 'Including, she arrived late' – 'including' cannot start a sentence as a discourse marker.

title: Multiple contexts (adding reason)
question_text: 'Inclusive, ela não me ligou.' – Best translation if the speaker is adding a reason for being upset?
opt3: On top of that
opt4: Including
explanation: Adding another reason strengthens the first point.

title: Clarification gap
sentence: ______, I was the one who broke the vase.
example3: Use a word that corrects a misunderstanding.

title: Surprising example gap
sentence: Everyone passed the exam, ______ the laziest student.
example2: This word introduces an extreme case.

title: Adding a point gap
sentence: The hotel room was small. ______, it was very expensive.
example4: Use a phrase that adds a stronger negative point.

title: Neutral list gap
sentence: We visited many cities, ______ Paris and London.

title: Besides gap
sentence: Let's stay home. ______, it's already dark outside.

title: Formal gap
sentence: The project is behind schedule. ______, the budget has been cut.
example3: Use a formal additive word suitable for reports.

title: Visualizing 'Actually' vs 'In fact'
prompt: 'Actually' is like gently raising a finger to say 'well, actually...' – polite correction. 'In fact' is like stamping a document – emphatic truth.

title: Visualizing 'Even' (surprise)
prompt: Imagine a group photo where even the grumpy cat is smiling – that's 'even'. It highlights the unexpected.
